AMD.headline: Data Center growth re-accelerated from +32% FY25 to +57% Q1 FY26; gross margin expanded 3pts on richer DC mix; MI325 China licenses secured at 25% US re-import tariff.

shifts.trend: DC revenue $16.6B FY25 (+32% YoY) → $5.8B Q1 FY26 (+57% YoY); DC now 56% of total revenue
shifts.note: Re-acceleration after years of GPU share losses to NVDA; OpenAI 6 GW deal provides multi-year visibility

shifts.trend: Gross margin 50% FY25 → 53% Q1 FY26 (+3pts); DC operating margin 22% FY25 → 28% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: DC mix shift is the primary driver; Client+Gaming dilutive but improving

shifts.trend: FY25: $800M MI308 China inventory charge, partial $360M reversal; Q1 FY26: Feb 2026 MI325 licenses granted with 25% US re-import tariff on shipments
shifts.note: MI308 impairment was the FY25 drag; MI325 licenses reduce the zero-revenue floor but 25% tariff caps volume economics

shifts.trend: ZT Systems closed $3.2B Mar 2025; Helios rack-scale platform retained; OpenAI warrant (160M shares) vests on MI450 milestones through Oct 2030
shifts.note: Capital-light model preserved: manufacturing arm sold to Sanmina for $2.4B; design/IP retained

AMD.verdict: DC growth re-acceleration and margin expansion are both thesis-confirming. China MI325 licenses reduce the zero-revenue floor. OpenAI 6 GW deal provides multi-year demand visibility. The remaining overhang is China tariffs (25%) limiting volume and ZT Systems integration execution.

AMAT.headline: Revenue flattened (-2% YoY Q1 FY26) after FY25 +4% as logic/foundry weak; DRAM/HBM mix jumped from 26% to 34% of Semiconductor Systems; Taiwan surged +46% while China fell to 30%; $253M legal settlement one-time.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $28.4B (+4%) → Q1 FY26 revenue $7.0B (-2% YoY); nine-month trend of logic/IDM softness offsetting memory strength
shifts.note: Trailing-edge logic weakness and DRAM investment time-lag drove the decel; not structural

shifts.trend: DRAM share of SemiSys 26% FY25 → 34% Q1 FY26 (+8pts) as HBM technology-transition spend front-loads; Foundry/logic fell to 62% from 69%
shifts.note: DRAM/HBM mix shift is the key positive — higher-intensity process control for HBM TSV

shifts.trend: China -16% YoY FY25 ($8.5B, 30% of rev) → China -7% YoY Q1 FY26; rate of decline moderating
shifts.note: Taiwan +46% in Q1 FY26 replacing China as the leading geography

shifts.trend: Gross margin 48.7% FY25 → 49.0% Q1 FY26; one-time $253M legal settlement excluded from gross margin
shifts.note: Operating margin Q1 FY26 26.1% (-4.3pts) includes the settlement; underlying gross margin stable

AMAT.verdict: AMAT is in a mixed cycle: memory/HBM strong and share growing, while logic/IDM spending and China are headwinds. The legal settlement ($253M) is one-time. If DRAM holds at 34%+ of SemiSys mix, margins improve as the leading-edge cycle completes. Net: thesis of HBM-driven WFE spend intact.

AMKR.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated from +6.2% FY25 to +27.5% Q1 2026; capex step-changed from $905M FY25 to $2.5-3B guided 2026 as Arizona advanced-packaging fab breaks ground.

shifts.trend: FY25 +6.2% → Q1 2026 +27.5% YoY; all four end markets (Communications +42%, Automotive +28%, Computing +19%, Consumer +4%) growing simultaneously
shifts.note: Prior year's Vietnam ramp inefficiency is now a tailwind as utilization rises

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M → Q1 2026 capex $224.6M vs $79.9M prior-year Q1 (+181%); FY26 guided $2.5-3.0B for Arizona Facility + advanced-packaging equipment
shifts.note: Arizona Facility (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing 2028) is a multi-year commitment; funded partly by $407M CHIPS Act direct funding

shifts.trend: GM 14.0% FY25 → 14.2% Q1 2026 (+2.3pts YoY) on higher factory utilization; partially offset by material costs
shifts.note: Vietnam ramp-related margin compression from FY25 unwinding as utilization recovers

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8% of FY25 net sales, Qualcomm 11.1%; top-10 = 72%; no change disclosed in Q1
shifts.note: Concentration risk unchanged — Apple remains the dominant customer at 30%

AMKR.verdict: The capex step-change is the key development — $2.5-3.0B committed for Arizona advanced packaging won't come online until 2028 but secures future CoWoS/HBM packaging capacity. Revenue re-acceleration (+27.5%) and margin expansion validate demand. Apple concentration (30%) remains the primary idiosyncratic risk.

AMZN.headline: AWS growth re-accelerated from +20% FY25 to +28% Q1 2026; Q1 capex $43.2B (+78% YoY) and RPO leapt from $244B to $364B; OpenAI $100B commitment expansion confirms AWS as primary AI training cloud.

shifts.trend: AWS $128.7B FY25 (+20%) → $37.6B Q1 2026 (+28% YoY); re-acceleration after several quarters of moderation
shifts.note: AWS operating margin also expanding: 35.4% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 2026 while capex surges

shifts.trend: FY25 cash capex $128.3B → Q1 2026 capex $43.2B (+78% YoY); on pace for $170B+ annualized
shifts.note: Both AWS technical infrastructure and fulfillment network increasing; 2026 capex expected to increase further

shifts.trend: AWS RPO $244B at FY25-end → $364B at Q1 2026-end; $120B RPO added in one quarter primarily from OpenAI $100B expansion
shifts.note: OpenAI expanded existing $38B AWS commitment by $100B over 8 years, includes Trainium chip obligations

shifts.trend: Amazon invested $15B in OpenAI Series C + committed another $35B ($50B total); AWS Anthropic deal: $5B more preferred + $20B financing facility post-Q1
shifts.note: AWS becoming the primary cloud for multiple frontier AI labs — structural demand lock-in

AMZN.verdict: AWS re-acceleration to +28% while operating margins expand to 37.7% and capex doubles is the ideal combination. The $364B RPO provides multi-year revenue visibility. OpenAI $100B deal + Anthropic $50B commitment lock in AWS as the dominant AI training cloud. Story intact and strengthening.

ANET.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +28.6% FY25 to +35.1% Q1 FY26; deferred revenue (acceptance-period Ethernet trials) ballooned from $5.37B to $6.20B in one quarter; gross margin compressed 220bps on large-customer mix and memory tightening.

shifts.trend: FY25 +28.6% → Q1 FY26 +35.1%; product +36.6% as AI Ethernet trials convert to committed orders
shifts.note: Cloud and AI Titans 48% of revenue; deferred revenue build ($6.20B) signals further conversion pipeline

shifts.trend: GM 64.1% FY25 → 61.9% Q1 FY26 (-220bps); memory/silicon input costs tightening newly cited as headwind in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Large-customer pricing discounts and silicon supply tightening are both compressing margins simultaneously

shifts.trend: Memory and silicon supply tightening newly cited in Q1 FY26 as gross-margin headwind; procurement discipline tightening; $413M committed inventory for Santa Clara new building
shifts.note: Memory tightening is a new risk not mentioned in FY25 10-K

shifts.trend: Americas revenue mix rose to 84.5% from 79.7%; two end customers were 26%/16% of FY25 revenue; deferred revenue $6.20B (+$826M in Q1) = lumpy recognition risk growing
shifts.note: RPO $7.7B at Q1-end with 91% expected in next 2 years; binding product orders $968M

ANET.verdict: AI Ethernet trials converting to committed orders at accelerating pace — the $6.20B deferred revenue buildup is structurally bullish. The new risks are gross margin compression from memory/silicon input costs and large-customer pricing. Thesis intact; watch gross margin for recovery as acceptance periods complete.

AVGO.headline: Revenue accelerated from +24% FY25 to +29% Q1 FY26; semiconductor solutions jumped +52% on custom AI accelerators (XPUs); top-5 customer concentration tightened from ~40% to ~50% in one quarter.

shifts.trend: FY25 +24% → Q1 FY26 +29%; semiconductor solutions +22% FY25 → +52% Q1 FY26 as custom XPU orders surge
shifts.note: Infrastructure software +26% FY25 → +1% Q1 FY26; AI semi ramp fully driving the beat

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers ~40% FY25 → ~50% Q1 FY26; one distributor 32% FY25 → 42% Q1 FY26 — implies single hyperscaler XPU customer driving the quarter
shifts.note: Highest concentration level disclosed to date; signals XPU cycle deepening at a specific hyperscaler

shifts.trend: Semi solutions 55% of FY25 revenue → 65% of Q1 FY26 revenue; AI ramp shifting mix away from software
shifts.note: Warning: AI rack/system leasing will 'compress gross margin' (FY25 10-K risk factor) — monitor as XPU racks scale

shifts.trend: GAAP gross margin 68% unchanged FY25 → Q1 FY26; software mix benefit offset by semi ramp cost; operating margin 40% FY25 → 44% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Operating margin improving on opex leverage; gross margin holding despite higher-volume semi mix

AVGO.verdict: AVGO's AI custom-ASIC business is compounding rapidly — +52% semi growth + tightening concentration at a single hyperscaler means the XPU cycle is deepening. The 65% semi revenue share and 50% top-5 concentration are both new highs. Investment question: can gross margins stay protected as XPU rack/system sales scale?

CDNS.headline: Revenue re-accelerated from +14% FY25 to +19% Q1 FY26; China revenue +36% YoY in Q1 FY26 as BIS EDA restrictions remain rescinded; backlog grew to $8.0B despite Hexagon D&E close.

shifts.trend: FY25 +14% → Q1 FY26 +19%; broad-based: Other Americas +87%, Japan +36%, China +36%, EMEA +23%
shifts.note: Only US was slow (+6%); international mix accelerating

shifts.trend: FY25 China $680M (+19%) → Q1 FY26 China $189.4M (+36% YoY, 13% of mix); BIS May/Jul 2025 restrictions were rescinded, China back to growth
shifts.note: September 2025 BIS 50%-owned-affiliate rule still flagged as a risk; but current environment is permissive

shifts.trend: Backlog $7.8B FY25 → $8.0B Q1 FY26; 53% to recognize in next 12mo (FY25) → 55% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Backlog growing and front-loading; solid revenue visibility

shifts.trend: Operating margin 28-29% consistently across both periods; GM held at ~90%; $200M Q1 FY26 repurchases vs $350M Q1 FY25
shifts.note: Hexagon D&E acquisition closed; revolving credit drawn; buyback pace reduced temporarily

CDNS.verdict: EDA fundamentals are solid with re-accelerating growth. The BIS China restrictions being rescinded removed the key overhang and China revenue is growing at 36% — faster than the company. Hexagon D&E adds engineering simulation exposure. Quality compounder thesis holds; not where AI cycle alpha lives.

CEG.headline: Calpine closed Jan 7 2026 — CEG is now the world's largest private power producer at 55 GW; nuclear PTC revenue declined YoY but long-term data-center PPAs building; Crane (TMI) restart secured $1B DOE loan guarantee.

shifts.trend: Scale: ~27 GW owned Q3 2025 → 55 GW post-Calpine Jan 2026; adds 21 GW gas + 730 MW geothermal + 800 MW battery
shifts.note: Calpine $22B acquisition; 2.5M retail accounts; 75% of Fortune 100 as customers

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted EPS $9.39 vs FY24 $8.67; but GAAP net income $2.32B vs $3.75B from lower nuclear PTCs
shifts.note: Nuclear PTC revenue declining as post-IRA market pricing normalizes

shifts.trend: Calpine acquisition $22B closed Jan 7 2026; Crane (TMI Unit 1) $1B DOE loan guarantee secured Nov 2025; Clinton nuclear 30 MW uprate by 2029
shifts.note: Meta 20-yr Clinton PPA (June 2025) and Microsoft 20-yr Crane PPA anchor the hyperscaler demand thesis

shifts.trend: NY PSC 20-year ZEC extension Jan 2026 for nuclear through 2049; 45Y clean electricity PTC under OBBBA supports nuclear economics
shifts.note: 80-year license extension applications intended across fleet; regulatory runway extended materially

CEG.verdict: The Calpine acquisition transformed CEG from a nuclear pure-play to a diversified clean-power producer — a 55 GW platform uniquely positioned for hyperscaler baseload PPAs. Nuclear PTC revenue decline is a near-term headwind. The thesis test is whether the combined platform can secure long-term data-center load contracts at premium pricing — Clinton/Microsoft and Meta/Calpine PPAs are early confirmations.

CLS.headline: CCS revenue growth accelerated from +42% FY25 to +76% Q1 FY26; Enterprise end-market rebounded +101% Q1 FY26 on next-gen AI/ML compute program ramp; revolver upsized from $750M to $1.75B for continued scale.

shifts.trend: CCS FY25 +42% → Q1 FY26 +76%; HPS networking switch revenue +81% FY25 → +63% Q1 FY26; total revenue Q1 +53% YoY
shifts.note: HPS growth rate normalizing (63% still very strong) but CCS acceleration driven by Enterprise rebound

shifts.trend: Enterprise end-market -19% FY25 (hyperscaler tech transition) → +101% Q1 FY26 (next-gen AI compute ramp); HPS 41% → 42% of total revenue
shifts.note: Enterprise +101% removes the prior-year drag; both networking (HPS) and compute (Enterprise) now growing

shifts.trend: Revolver upsized from $750M to $1.75B; Term A refinanced with maturity extended to April 2031; balance sheet positioned for continued scale
shifts.note: Debt capacity expansion signals management confidence in multi-quarter revenue growth

shifts.trend: CCS segment margin 8.4% Q4 FY25 → 8.6% Q1 FY26; ATS 6.0%; adjusted EPS $2.16 beat high end of $1.95-2.15 guide
shifts.note: Segment margins improving on operating leverage despite rapid revenue ramp

CLS.verdict: Celestica is executing the hyperscaler networking switch ramp cleanly. The Enterprise rebound (+101% Q1) removes the prior-year drag. HPS business (42% of revenue) at +63% YoY with margin expansion is the core thesis driver. No new risks surfaced — story intact.

COHR.headline: Datacenter & Communications growth accelerated from +34% Q2 to +41% Q3; 'scale across' interconnect named as new demand category in Q3; sequential margin expansion continues as divestitures narrow portfolio to AI optics.

shifts.trend: D&C +34% Q2 FY26 → +41% Q3 FY26; total revenue +17% → +21%; 9-month D&C revenue $3.66B +34% YoY
shifts.note: Six consecutive quarters of D&C revenue growth acceleration — not a one-quarter anomaly

shifts.trend: Q3 first explicit mention of 'scale across' (inter-DC AI fabric) as growth driver alongside DCI and telecom; Industrial -16% on Munich and aerospace divestitures
shifts.note: 'Scale across' broadens the optical demand addressable market from cluster (scale-out/up) to inter-DC fabric

shifts.trend: D&C segment profit +44% Q2 → +49% Q3; gross margin 37% Q2 → 38% Q3 (+243bps YoY); interest expense declining on term-loan paydown
shifts.note: Structural margin re-rating as AI optics mix dominates and divestitures narrow the portfolio

shifts.trend: Munich Germany and aerospace/defense divestitures completed; portfolio narrowing to AI/DC optics and DCI; lower interest expense -$13M Q3, -$39M YTD
shifts.note: Portfolio simplification is a structural positive — removes margin-dilutive legacy segments

COHR.verdict: COHR's AI datacenter thesis is accelerating — growth rate and gross margin moving together in the right direction. The new 'scale across' commentary broadens addressable demand beyond the cluster. Divestiture-narrowing is a positive structural move. Story intact and strengthening.

CRM.headline: Growth stable at 9-10%; RPO dipped from $63.4B (Jan 2025) to $59.5B (Oct 2025) then recovered to $72.4B (Jan 2026, +14% YoY); Informatica ($9.6B) closed Nov 2025; cRPO +16% signals near-term re-acceleration.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 +9% → FY26 +10%; Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack +19% the only double-digit growth segment in Q3
shifts.note: Steady low-double-digit growth; Agentforce AI consumption pricing is the optionality not yet in the run-rate

shifts.trend: RPO $63.4B Jan 2025 → $59.5B Oct 2025 (declined mid-year) → $72.4B Jan 2026 (+14% YoY); cRPO +16% outpacing total RPO
shifts.note: RPO recovery + cRPO acceleration signals near-term demand improving heading into FY27

shifts.trend: Informatica acquired $9.6B Nov 2025 (contributed $0.4B FY26 revenue); Agentforce branding consolidated across all products in Q3 FY26
shifts.note: M&A was funded from operating cash flow ($15.0B FY26) plus modest debt

shifts.trend: Repurchased $12.7B (50M shares) + $1.6B dividends; operating cash flow $15.0B (+15%); EPS $7.80 vs $6.36
shifts.note: Best-in-class capital returns for an enterprise SaaS compounder

CRM.verdict: CRM is a steady 10% grower with solid cash returns. The Agentforce pivot is credible but monetization is early-stage. RPO recovery to $72.4B and cRPO +16% are encouraging leading indicators. Not where fast AI cycle money lives; the consumption-based Agentforce pricing is optionality worth tracking.

CRWV.headline: RPO surged from $60.7B to $98.8B in one quarter; Microsoft concentration fell from 67% to 45% as Meta emerged at 20%; revenue +112% Q1 2026; debt pile escalated to $21.6B+ with another $7.8B raised post-quarter.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5.1B (+168%) → Q1 2026 $2.08B (+112% YoY); annualized Q1 pace ≈$8.3B — strongly above FY25
shifts.note: YoY growth rate normalizing from hypergrowth base, but absolute revenue accelerating

shifts.trend: Microsoft 67% FY25 revenue → 45% Q1 2026; Meta emerged as 20% customer; moving from 1-customer to 2-customer story
shifts.note: Diversification is structurally important — reduces Microsoft renegotiation risk

shifts.trend: RPO $60.7B Dec 2025 → $98.8B Mar 2026 (+63% in one quarter); 36% recognized in next 24 months, 39% in months 25-48
shifts.note: RPO growth rate exceeding revenue growth — committed backlog building faster than recognition

shifts.trend: Debt $21.6B FY25 → post-Q1 add: $4.0B converts + $2.8B senior notes + $1.0B equity = ~$29.4B+ total debt; Q1 net loss $740M vs $1.2B FY25 full-year
shifts.note: Debt-to-revenue ratio remains stretched; profitability path dependent on RPO conversion at high margins

CRWV.verdict: CRWV is growing into its debt load — the RPO/revenue trajectory is strong but leverage at $29B+ with widening net losses is the dominant risk. Diversification away from Microsoft toward Meta is structurally important. Thesis holds as long as hyperscaler capital commitments stay intact; the leverage is the key fragility.

CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated from +15% Q1 to +21% Q2 on AI Infrastructure demand; Security declining -4% on Splunk subscription transition; Silicon One purchase commitments building as hyperscaler capacity secured.

shifts.trend: Networking +15% Q1 FY26 → +21% Q2 FY26; total revenue +8% → +10%; 1H FY26 revenue $30.23B +9%
shifts.note: AI Infrastructure and Data Center Switching leading the Networking acceleration

shifts.trend: Security -4% Q2 on Splunk on-prem to cloud subscription transition and prior-gen product declines; trend expected to continue H2 FY26
shifts.note: Splunk revenue model shift is a known multi-quarter headwind; not structural deterioration

shifts.trend: Silicon One purchase commitments: inventory +7% and purchase commitments +10% vs FYE 2025, explicitly tied to hyperscaler AI Infrastructure orders; mgmt flags obsolescence risk
shifts.note: Commitment to Silicon One contract manufacturing signals multi-quarter AI demand confidence

shifts.trend: Product GM -0.6pts Q1 → +0.2pts Q2 on productivity offsets; oscillating around neutral
shifts.note: No structural margin compression — mix and productivity offsetting each other

CSCO.verdict: AI Infrastructure Networking accelerating at +21% with Silicon One commitment building. Security drag from Splunk subscription transition is a known headwind expected to persist into H2 FY26. Net: story intact on networking; Security creates a cap on headline growth rates.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas Q1 FY26 customer orders +42% organic with backlog reaching $14.5B (+44% YoY); data center primary driver in both segments; Boyd Thermal ($9.5B liquid cooling) pending close extends Eaton from grid to chip.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas organic +16% FY25 → Q1 FY26 customer orders +42% organic; Electrical Global +21% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Order growth consistently outpacing revenue growth — backlog build pointing to future acceleration

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas backlog $13.2B (+31% organic) FY25 → $14.5B (+44% YoY) Q1 FY26; total company backlog $19.8B FY25
shifts.note: Book-to-bill 1.2x in Americas; strong deferred revenue pipeline

shifts.trend: Boyd Thermal acquisition $9.5B announced Nov 2025 (pending close); extends Eaton into liquid cooling — chip-to-grid portfolio from power to cooling
shifts.note: Fibrebond (modular DC enclosures) and Resilient (solid-state transformers) also acquired in FY25

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas operating margin 29.9% FY25 → 25.6% Q1 FY26 (-440bps); commodity/tariff inflation eating operating leverage
shifts.note: Q1 FY26 compression is the key risk — if input costs persist, backlog will ship at lower margin

ETN.verdict: Backlog and order growth are thesis-confirming. The margin compression (29.9% → 25.6%) is the risk — commodity/tariff inflation is outpacing price leverage in Q1. Boyd Thermal acquisition is strategically important for the full chip-to-grid cooling portfolio. If margins recover as volumes ship, thesis strengthens; if cost inflation persists, it's a margin headwind story.

FN.headline: DCI revenue surged from $142M Q2 to $197M Q3 (+90.4% YoY) as inter-DC AI scale-across fabric ramps; total revenue growth accelerated from +35.9% to +39.3%; customer concentration broadened to 4 customers each >10%.

shifts.note: DCI is now the fastest-growing optical product line, overtaking Telecom on a YoY-growth basis

shifts.trend: HPC non-optical $85.6M Q2 → $106.7M Q3 (+$21M QoQ); 9-month HPC $207.7M — becoming a material new line
shifts.note: HPC line (AI training hardware optical interconnects) represents a new revenue stream alongside traditional optical

shifts.trend: 2 customers >10% Q2 (combined 48.5%) → 4 customers >10% Q3 (combined 59.1%); first time at 4-customer concentration
shifts.note: Concentration tightening reflects the underlying AI demand concentration — but raises idiosyncratic risk

shifts.trend: GM 12.2% Q2 → 11.9% Q3 (-30bps); operating margin 10.1% → 9.9%; operating cash flow Q2 $148.8M 6M (vs $199M prior) on inventory build
shifts.note: Thin margins are structural for a contract manufacturer; inventory build signals demand confidence

FN.verdict: DCI growth (+90.4% YoY) and HPC line expansion are the standout data points — Fabrinet is riding the 1.6T-cycle inter-DC fabric buildout. Concentration tightening (4 customers at 59%) adds idiosyncratic risk but reflects the underlying AI demand concentration. Story intact.

GEV.headline: RPO grew from $150.2B FY25 to $163.3B Q1 2026 (+9% QoQ, +32% YoY); HA-Turbine orders 43 in FY25 → 12 Q1 2026 (on pace for 48+); free cash flow $4.8B Q1 vs $1.0B prior year; Prolec GE transformer JV acquired.

shifts.trend: FY25 +9% organic revenue → Q1 2026 +16%; Power segment EBITDA +57% Q1 on favorable pricing and volume
shifts.note: HA-Turbine deliveries and favorable pricing driving EBITDA margin expansion

shifts.trend: Total RPO $150.2B FY25 → $163.3B Q1 2026 (+$13B sequentially, +$39.8B YoY); equipment RPO $64.2B → $75.9B (+18%)
shifts.note: Heavy-duty gas turbine orders 110 units FY25 vs 68 FY24 vs 41 FY23 — structural demand acceleration

shifts.trend: Prolec GE (50% stake in 10,000-employee transformer JV) acquired Feb 2026 for ~$5.3B; Electrification RPO growing from switchgear/HVDC/transformer demand
shifts.note: Transformer acquisition perfectly timed with data-center power infrastructure bottleneck

shifts.trend: Power segment EBITDA margin 14.7% FY25 → 16.3% Q1 2026 (+160bps); total EBITDA $896M Q1 (+$439M YoY)
shifts.note: Pricing power from multi-year supply lead times enabling above-normal margin expansion

GEV.verdict: GEV is compounding RPO and margins simultaneously — $163B booked backlog at 16% segment margins and growing. The Prolec GE acquisition adds transformer capacity at exactly the right moment. Gas Power demand data (turbine orders 41→68→110) shows structural, not cyclical, acceleration. Thesis intact and one of the strongest in the power layer.

GNRC.headline: Recovered from -2% FY25 to +12% Q1 FY26; C&I segment (data center gensets) from +4.9% FY25 to +28% Q1 FY26; Enercon Engineering acquired for hyperscale custom power; C&I margin expanded from 11.4% to 13.0%.

shifts.trend: FY25 total -2% (residential softness on quiet 2025 outage season) → Q1 FY26 +12%; recovery led by C&I segment
shifts.note: Residential flat +1% Q1 FY26 as higher pricing offsets lower volumes; hurricane-season recovery optionality intact

shifts.trend: C&I +4.9% FY25 (data center and telecom) → C&I +28% Q1 FY26 (core growth driven by global data center customers); Residential +1% Q1 FY26 stable
shifts.note: Segment reorganization Mar 2026: now Residential/C&I (vs prior Domestic/International) — cleaner AI exposure disclosure

shifts.trend: C&I margin 11.4% Q1 FY25 → 13.0% Q1 FY26; Residential margin 20.3% → 25.1%; adjusted EBITDA +29%
shifts.note: C&I margin expansion on price/cost and Allmand (acquisition) accretion

shifts.trend: Large-megawatt DC backup generator market 'significantly supply-constrained' (FY25 10-K); Enercon Engineering acquired for custom hyperscale power equipment
shifts.note: Supply constraint in large-megawatt gensets is a structural opportunity for Generac to scale

GNRC.verdict: Recovery trajectory is clear — C&I data center demand (+28%) is now the story rather than residential. C&I margin expansion (11.4% → 13.0%) and Enercon acquisition add hyperscale credentials. The large-megawatt backup generator market being 'supply-constrained' is the thesis catalyst. Residential drag persists until the next hurricane season.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud accelerated from +36% FY25 to +63% Q1 2026; Q1 capex $35.7B on pace for $143B+ annual; revenue backlog leapt from $242.8B to $467.6B as multi-GW TPU supply agreements were disclosed for the first time.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud +36% FY25 ($58.7B) → +63% Q1 2026 ($20.0B); Cloud op income $13.9B FY25 → $6.6B Q1 2026 (3x YoY at 33% margin)
shifts.note: Cloud margin expanding even as capex surges — proving the infrastructure investment is generating high-return workloads

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $91.4B (vs $52.5B FY24, +74%) → Q1 2026 capex $35.7B alone (+79% YoY); 2026 to 'significantly increase' relative to 2025
shifts.note: Full-year 2026 capex pacing $143B+ — nearly double FY25's record; servers, network equipment, data centers

shifts.trend: Revenue backlog $242.8B FY25 → $467.6B Q1 2026 (+93%); $462.3B is Google Cloud; first-time disclosure of multi-GW TPU hardware supply agreements to third-party DCs
shifts.note: GOOGL is now selling TPU capacity to third-party data centers — a category shift from cloud services to hardware infrastructure

shifts.trend: Committed $40B to a private company (Anthropic-like deal) in March 2026; Wiz $29.5B and Intersect $5.9B closed
shifts.note: AI ecosystem investment deepening — GOOGL building a multi-decade competitive moat via AI lab partnerships

GOOGL.verdict: The new disclosure — GOOGL selling multi-gigawatt TPU capacity to third parties — is a category shift: GOOGL is monetizing AI infrastructure directly (not just through software). Cloud margin expansion to 33% while capex doubles is thesis-upgrading. The $467.6B backlog is the strongest demand signal in the hyperscaler group.

HPE.headline: Networking grew +51.1% FY25 (Juniper post-close) → +151.5% Q1 FY26 (full-quarter Juniper); Cloud & AI (servers) declined -2.7% as GPU-heavy AI mix carries 'limited margins'; memory component shortages flagged as new Q1 risk.

shifts.trend: Networking +51.1% FY25 ($2.1B Juniper contribution) → +151.5% Q1 FY26 (full quarter Juniper); total revenue +13.8% → +18.4%
shifts.note: Juniper base-effect drives the headline; underlying networking market demand also strong

shifts.trend: Cloud & AI (new combined Server+Storage+Financial Services segment) -2.7% Q1 FY26 despite AI-server demand; GPU pass-through pricing carries 'limited margins'
shifts.note: AI server paradox: demand strong but revenue declining and margins limited from GPU pricing structure

shifts.trend: Non-GAAP operating margin 9.8% FY25 → 12.7% Q1 FY26 on Juniper revenue mix; Juniper integration costs $123M Q1 still being absorbed
shifts.note: Path to $600M Juniper synergies by FY28 requires $800M investment; incremental margin capture already visible

shifts.trend: Memory component shortages newly flagged as Q1 FY26 risk; silicon tightening also cited alongside AI server demand
shifts.note: Memory shortage is a new supply-chain risk not mentioned in FY25 10-K

HPE.verdict: Juniper integration is delivering on margins (+280bps non-GAAP in one quarter). The AI server mix paradox (demand strong, revenue declining, margins limited from GPU pass-through) is the key tension. Memory/silicon shortages add a new supply-chain risk. $600M synergy target by FY28 remains the long-term value driver — on track.

HUBB.headline: Electrical Solutions organic growth improved from +7.2% FY25 to +10.6% Q1 FY26 with datacenter consistently the primary volume driver; backlog $2.16B at YE25; Utility Solutions +6.8% organic Q1 on T&D demand.

shifts.trend: Electrical Solutions organic +7.2% FY25 → +10.6% Q1 FY26; Utility Solutions organic +1.1% FY25 → +6.8% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Broad acceleration across both segments; datacenter and T&D both contributing

shifts.trend: Datacenter and light industrial cited as primary volume growth driver in both FY25 and Q1 FY26; heavy industrial soft in both periods
shifts.note: Consistent datacenter demand signal across two reporting periods

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted op margin 22.5% (+80bps); Q1 FY26 Electrical Solutions adjusted 16.4% (-30bps from tariff/commodity), Utility Solutions 21.8% (+190bps)
shifts.note: Mixed margin picture: commodity/tariff inflation pressuring Electrical, favorable pricing in Utility

HUBB.verdict: HUBB is a steady data-center-exposed electrical components compounder — consistent 7-11% Electrical Solutions organic growth with datacenter as a secular driver alongside T&D grid buildout. Not high-beta, but durable. The tariff/commodity inflation pressure on Electrical margins is the near-term watch item.

INTC.headline: DCAI re-accelerated to +22% Q1 2026 on supply-constrained premium ASPs; Intel Foundry external revenue only $174M Q1 with $2.4B operating loss; 14A future contingent on landing a significant external customer.

shifts.trend: DCAI: FY25 +5.6% YoY → Q1 2026 +22% YoY on +27% higher server ASPs from premium mix and demand-based pricing; CCG +1% FY25 → +1% Q1 2026 (flat)
shifts.note: ASP-driven: server volume actually -5% Q1 as supply constrained; premium mix + demand-based pricing driving DCAI

shifts.trend: 'Market demand exceeded available product supply in Q1 2026 due to internal and external supply constraints' — same constraint language in both FY25 and Q1 filings; expected to persist H1 2026 per Q1
shifts.note: Intel 7 wafer + substrate + memory all constraining; no easing language added in Q1

shifts.trend: Intel Foundry external revenue $307M FY25 → $174M Q1 2026 (Altera now external); Foundry operating loss $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 (ratio comparable at ~$9.5B annualized)
shifts.note: 18A ramping to high-volume production but still primarily internal; external revenue tiny

shifts.trend: NVIDIA $5B strategic partnership + SoftBank $2B equity (Sep 2025) + CHIPS Act $6.4B direct funding validate 18A; DOJ SCIP equity buyout completes Q2 2026
shifts.note: Strategic investors validating 18A is the most important external signal; CHIPS Act locks in government support

INTC.verdict: DCAI re-acceleration (+22%) driven by ASP premium is near-term thesis-supportive, but it's supply-constrained volume that limits upside. The binary question: can Intel 14A land a significant external foundry customer? Without one, management may pause leading-edge process development — a binary outcome for the turnaround.

KLAC.headline: Revenue accelerated from +7% Q2 to +11% Q3 (record quarter); Korea (memory/HBM) surged from +34% to +80%; China share contracted from 30.2% to 24%; gross margin -30bps from DRAM chip costs — management says transitory.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 $3.30B (+7% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $3.42B (+11% YoY); record revenue in Q3; 9-month $10.14B +9% YoY
shifts.note: Process control intensity rising with 2nm node investment and HBM TSV complexity

shifts.trend: Korea +34% Q2 → +80% Q3 as HBM investment front-loads; Taiwan -12% Q3 on shipment timing but 9-month flat; North America +38% Q2 → +40% Q3
shifts.note: Korea surge is HBM DRAM technology-transition investment; Taiwan timing normalization expected

shifts.trend: China 30.2% of Q2 revenue (-9% YoY) → 24% of Q3 revenue (+5% YoY); 9-month China $3.09B flat
shifts.note: China deconcentration from 36% to 24% over two quarters reduces geopolitical exposure

shifts.trend: GM 61.4% Q2 → 61.1% Q3 (-30bps) from escalating DRAM chip costs for image computers; management calls it transitory
shifts.note: Memory chip costs for KLA's own image-computer equipment are the compression driver — not product pricing

KLAC.verdict: Record revenue + Korea/HBM investment front-loading are thesis-confirming. GM compression from DRAM chip costs is a transitory supply-driven cost headwind. China deconcentration (30% → 24%) reduces geopolitical risk. 17th consecutive annual dividend increase signals management confidence. Story intact.

LEU.headline: Backlog grew from $3.8B to $3.9B; $900M DOE HALEU task order won Jan 2026; Fluor EPC contract signed Feb 2026; exploring JV with Oklo on HALEU deconversion at Pike County.

shifts.trend: Backlog $3.8B FY25 → $3.9B Q1 2026; LEU segment $2.9B → $3.1B (incl. $2.3-2.4B contingent contracts pending new capacity); SWU spot $200 vs $34 historical low
shifts.note: Contingent contracts converting to definitive as capacity expansion de-risks

shifts.trend: Advanced technology costs $3.0M Q1 2025 → $18.9M Q1 2026 (+530%); Fluor EPC contract signed Feb 2026 for Piketon LEU+HALEU expansion; Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing started
shifts.note: Execution accelerating: EPC signed, centrifuge manufacturing started — both de-risk the capacity expansion

shifts.trend: $900M DOE HALEU task order Jan 5, 2026; $62.4M IRS 48C credit; EPC with Fluor locked; Dec 2025 DOE HALEU task order cascade started
shifts.note: Centrus is the only NRC-licensed HALEU producer with active enrichment — regulatory moat intact

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 revenue $76.7M (+5% YoY); LEU segment -13% on lower SWU volume offset by +52% higher ASPs
shifts.note: Revenue lumpy by design (SWU deliveries) — the backlog/pipeline is the primary business signal, not quarterly revenue

LEU.verdict: LEU is a nuclear fuel enrichment buildout story with regulatory moat (only licensed HALEU producer). The $900M DOE task order and Fluor EPC contract significantly de-risk expansion. SWU spot at $200 (vs $34 historical low) validates structural scarcity. Oklo JV on HALEU deconversion at Pike County adds complementary optionality.

LITE.headline: Revenue surged from +65.5% Q2 to +90.1% Q3; gross margin expanded from 36.1% to 44.2% (+1,540bps YoY); supply allocation language hardened between filings; cloud transceivers +$137M and optical circuit switches contributing $25M+ Q3.

shifts.note: Acceleration despite a large base — the 1.6T cycle is expanding not plateauing

shifts.trend: GM 36.1% Q2 FY26 → 44.2% Q3 FY26 (+1,540bps YoY); 9-month GM 38.8% (+1,300bps); operating leverage from supply allocation + 200G-lane mix
shifts.note: Structural not cyclical re-rating — 200G-lane (the 1.6T building block) carries structurally higher ASPs and margins

shifts.trend: Q2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation' → Q3: 'has required us to make decisions on supply allocation'; language hardened
shifts.note: Allocation language hardening = pricing discipline holding; implies pricing power remains intact

shifts.trend: 2 customers at 24%/17% Q2 → 2 customers at 26%/12% Q3; top customer rising from 24% to 26%
shifts.note: Concentration tightening but not alarmingly — still 2-customer story rather than 1-customer

LITE.verdict: LITE is the single clearest InP/EML supply choke point for the 1.6T transceiver cycle. Gross margins re-rating +1,540bps YoY to 44.2% while growth accelerates to +90.1% is as strong a signal as exists in the AI infrastructure stack. Supply allocation language hardening each quarter means pricing discipline holds. Thesis intact and strengthening.

LRCX.headline: Sequential revenue grew +9% QoQ from $5.34B to $5.84B on DRAM investment surge; memory mix jumped from 34% to 39% while Foundry fell from 59% to 54%; Korea surged to 23% as HBM investment front-loads.

shifts.trend: Dec 2025 revenue $5.34B → Mar 2026 $5.84B (+9% QoQ); 9-month revenue $16.51B (+24% YoY)
shifts.note: DRAM customer investments the explicit driver; six-month trend shows +24% YoY consistently

shifts.trend: Memory mix 34% Dec → 39% Mar (DRAM/NVM investment); Foundry 59% Dec → 54% Mar (mature-node weakness); Korea 20% → 23%
shifts.note: DRAM/HBM mix rising is structurally positive for LRCX — HBM stacking requires more deposition and etch steps per wafer

shifts.trend: GM 49.6% Dec → 49.8% Mar (+20bps QoQ); net income $1.83B (+15% QoQ); factory efficiencies offsetting unfavorable customer mix
shifts.note: Gross margin stability despite mix shift to memory (historically lower margin) suggests product/service mix improving

shifts.trend: China 35% Dec → 34% Mar; Korea 20% → 23%; China deconcentrating slowly
shifts.note: China stable at 34-35%; not a new headwind but still the largest single geography

LRCX.verdict: LRCX's WFE thesis is playing out — DRAM/HBM investment is the growth driver with memory mix rising to 39%. Foundry declining share reflects mature-node slowdown, not a structural problem. Korea surging to 23% confirms HBM investment is front-loading. Story intact; next catalyst is leading-edge foundry recovery.

META.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated from +22% FY25 to +33% Q1 2026; 2026 capex guidance raised to $125-145B (from $115-135B); $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments plus $182.9B in uncommenced lease obligations frame the true scale of the AI bet.

shifts.trend: FY25 +22% → Q1 2026 +33%; ad impressions +12% → +19%; ad price per impression +9% → +12%
shifts.note: Both volume and pricing accelerating simultaneously — Reels monetization and AI ad targeting driving the beat

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $72.2B → 2026 guide raised to $125-145B (from $115-135B at YE); Q1 capex $19.8B on pace for $79B+ annualized
shifts.note: FY26 capex guide raised $10B in first quarter — confidence in AI demand is strengthening

shifts.trend: Non-cancelable commitments $237.7B at Mar 31 + $182.9B off-balance-sheet uncommenced leases; $42.3B due 2026 and $47.7B in 2027
shifts.note: Total committed AI infrastructure spend exceeds $420B — META is the largest single infrastructure buyer in the AI cycle

shifts.trend: Reality Labs op loss $19.2B FY25; Q1 RL loss $4.03B; full-year 2026 expected similar to FY25
shifts.note: RL is a persistent drag but no new news; Horizon/XR ecosystem not materially changing

META.verdict: META's AI bet is scaling rapidly — revenue re-acceleration to +33% while capex guidance raised to $125-145B is a massive commitment. The $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments plus $182.9B in leases = ~$420B pre-committed to AI infrastructure. Reality Labs losses are a persistent but stable drag. Thesis as the largest demand-side AI infrastructure buyer is intact.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions accelerated from +24% Q2 to +51% Q3; segment margin compressed in both quarters on DC capacity ramp costs; Gentherm Reverse Morris Trust announced in Jan 2026 — Modine becomes pure-play DC cooling.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions net sales +24% Q2 FY26 → +51% Q3 FY26; DC product sales +$67M Q2 → +$130M Q3 YoY; 9-month DC product growth +$221.7M YoY
shifts.note: Hyperscale and colocation in NA and Europe cited as drivers in both filings

shifts.trend: Performance Technologies spin-merge with Gentherm announced Jan 2026 (Reverse Morris Trust, ~$1B value); MOD keeps Climate Solutions as pure-play DC+HVAC company
shifts.note: Structural positive — removes industrial/auto diversification discount; $210M cash to MOD at close (targeted end CY26)

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions operating margin 17.6% Q2 → 15.3% Q3; ramp inefficiencies from rapid DC capacity expansion in both quarters
shifts.note: New DC manufacturing lease signed Q3; capacity ramp costs are near-term, not structural

shifts.trend: Q2: $59.4M H1 capex + incremental $100M investment announced over next 12 months; Q3: new data-center manufacturing lease added
shifts.note: Each quarter adds new DC capacity commitment — confidence in multi-year demand is growing

MOD.verdict: The Gentherm RMT is strategically transformative — market has undervalued MOD inside an industrial conglomerate. Post-transaction, MOD is a pure-play DC cooling company with 51% segment growth. Margin compression (17.6% → 15.3%) is the cost of capacity buildout. If margins recover post-ramp, the re-rating argument strengthens. Thesis intact.

MSFT.headline: Azure growth accelerated from +39% Q2 to +40% Q3; quarterly capex $29.9B → $30.9B (stable massive build); commercial RPO stable at $625-627B; Cloud GM declining on AI infrastructure investments.

shifts.trend: Azure +39% Q2 FY26 → +40% Q3 FY26; Microsoft Cloud $51.5B Q2 (+26%) → $54.5B Q3 (+29%); 9-month MS Cloud $155.1B (+27%)
shifts.note: Re-acceleration of Azure growth after several quarters of stabilization — confirms AI workload demand is real

shifts.trend: Q2 capex $29.9B → Q3 capex $30.9B; 9-month $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year (2x pace maintained)
shifts.note: Steady-state of $30B/quarter capex sustained for two consecutive quarters — structural, not a spike

shifts.trend: Commercial RPO $625B Q2 → $627B Q3; flat despite significant revenue recognition — new bookings matching recognition
shifts.note: RPO stability at $625-627B signals underlying demand matching recognition: no backlog deterioration

shifts.trend: Microsoft Cloud GM declined (67% Q2 noted) on AI infrastructure investments; 9M operating income $5.9B non-operating OpenAI recapitalization gain
shifts.note: Cloud GM compression is the expected trade-off from massive AI infra spend; expected to trough as capacity monetizes

MSFT.verdict: Azure re-accelerating to +40% while the RPO holds at $625-627B and capex maintains $30B/quarter pace is a steady-state AI infrastructure build of unprecedented scale. Cloud GM compression is a transitory cost of AI capacity build. The OpenAI recapitalization gain is one-time. Story intact.

MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

MRVL.headline: Data Center grew from 73% to 74% of revenue mix with +38% to +46% YoY growth; Distributor A concentration rose from 34% to 37%; Celestial AI + XConn acquisitions closed post year-end to add photonic fabric and CXL/PCIe switching.

shifts.trend: DC end-market +38% Q3 FY26 → +46% FY26; overall revenue +37% Q3 → +42% FY26
shifts.note: Communications recovering +31% FY26 (vs inventory-correction negative trend in prior periods)

shifts.trend: Distributor A 34% Q3 FY26 → 37% FY26; Direct Customer A 11% → 14%; hyperscaler XPU concentration rising
shifts.note: Tightest concentration yet reported — implies single hyperscaler XPU cycle is deepening

shifts.trend: Celestial AI ~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares (photonic fabric, closed Feb 2026) + XConn $280M (CXL/PCIe switching, Feb 2026)
shifts.note: Roadmap now spans SerDes + ARM compute + silicon photonics + CPO + CXL switching at 5nm/3nm/2nm/1.4nm

shifts.trend: FY26: DC $6.1B (74% of mix, +46%); Communications $2.1B (+31%); enterprise/carrier/auto consolidated into 'Communications and other' for simpler reporting
shifts.note: Segment consolidation signals DC is the only business that matters; everything else is noise

MRVL.verdict: MRVL's custom AI ASIC trajectory continues to strengthen. Celestial AI + XConn acquisitions add CPO/photonic layer and CXL switching — meaningful roadmap differentiation. Concentration at Distributor A (37%) is the main risk. With 2nm/1.4nm designs already in development, the 5-year roadmap is credible.

MXL.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +30% FY25 to +43% Q1 2026; customer concentration improved (top customer 20%+ FY25 → 13% Q1 2026); Infrastructure (optical DSP) confirmed as growth driver alongside broadband recovery.

shifts.trend: FY25 +30% → Q1 2026 +43% YoY; Infrastructure +$36.3M Q1 2026; Broadband +$87.6M FY25 (+59%)
shifts.note: Recovery from FY24 inventory correction is complete; both broadband and optical DSP now growing

shifts.trend: Top customer 20%+ FY25 → 13% Q1 2026; top-10 65% → 56%; customer base broadening
shifts.note: Improved diversification reduces idiosyncratic customer risk

shifts.trend: Infrastructure (optical DSPs, wireless backhaul/access) explicitly cited as Q1 growth driver; 800G → 1.6T switch interconnect transition described as next-leg opportunity
shifts.note: Optical DSP cycle aligning with LITE/COHR/FN thesis on 1.6T transceiver cycle

shifts.trend: GM 57% FY25 (+3pts YoY); lower intangible amortization and favorable mix vs inventory-correction year
shifts.note: GM recovery from the FY24 trough — cyclical improvement, not structural change

MXL.verdict: MXL's recovery is real — +43% Q1 growth with broadening customer base and improving margins. The optical DSP business (Infrastructure segment) confirms the 800G→1.6T transition thesis. Small-cap with binary risk from Broadband cyclicality, but the optical DSP cycle is in early innings.

NOW.headline: Revenue stable at +21-22%; RPO $28.2B FY25 → $27.7B Q1 2026 (timing-driven); cRPO +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 2026 (modest moderation); Now Assist embedded across all major product lines.

shifts.trend: FY25 +21% → Q1 2026 +22%; subscription revenue 97% of total both periods
shifts.note: Consistent mid-20% growth for 4+ consecutive quarters — highly durable compounder

shifts.trend: Total RPO $28.2B FY25 → $27.7B Q1 2026 (slight timing dip); cRPO +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Minor moderation in cRPO growth (25% → 23%) is within noise — underlying demand stable

shifts.trend: Self-hosted US federal deliveries $492M FY25 → $138M Q1 2026 ($552M annualized pace); Now Assist embedded across ITSM/ITOM/CSM/HRSD/FSM/SecOps
shifts.note: Federal self-hosted expanding but consumption-based AI agent pricing not yet in the run-rate growth

NOW.verdict: NOW is a steady 21-22% growth compounder with durable RPO and deepening Now Assist AI-agent embed. No thesis-breaking changes. Not high-beta; the consumption-pricing optionality from Now Assist is the long-term upside not yet reflected in steady 22% growth rates.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

NVT.headline: Growth accelerated from +30% FY25 (organic +13%) to +53.5% Q1 FY26 (organic +34.4%); Systems Protection organic +50.1% Q1 on infrastructure/data center; segment margin expanded to 22.7% from 20.7%.

shifts.trend: FY25 +30% total (organic +12.6%) → Q1 FY26 +53.5% total (organic +34.4%); step-function acceleration
shifts.note: Avail Infrastructure Solutions (EPG) closed May 2025 contributing ~$121M Systems Protection Q1; both organic and inorganic accelerating

shifts.trend: Systems Protection organic ~17% FY25 → 50.1% Q1 FY26; ~46pts from infrastructure/datacenter in Q1; Electrical Connections +15.3% Q1
shifts.note: Infrastructure vertical (enclosures + liquid cooling for data centers) is now >50% organic growth — fastest segment in the portfolio

shifts.trend: Systems Protection segment margin 20.7% FY25 → 22.7% Q1 FY26 (+200bps); consolidated operating margin 15.8% stable
shifts.note: Volume leverage driving Systems Protection margin expansion even as consolidated margins held by gross-margin headwinds

shifts.trend: $1.0B Avail EPG acquisition (DC enclosures, power distribution) closed May 2025; Boyd Thermal ($9.5B ETN deal) is peer context for liquid cooling acquisitions
shifts.note: EPG is already contributing $121M/quarter; liquid cooling organic growth adds to the portfolio

NVT.verdict: NVT's growth re-acceleration (+34% organic Q1) combined with Systems Protection segment margin expansion to 22.7% is strongly thesis-confirming. Data center enclosures and liquid cooling thesis playing out. EPG acquisition contributing meaningfully. Story intact and strengthening.

OKLO.headline: Order book at ~18,100 MWe (+2,500% since SPAC 2023); Meta prepayment agreement Jan 2026 for 1.2 GW Pike County — first hyperscaler prepayment for advanced fission; FY25 opex $139.3M with FY26 guided $80-100M operating + $350-450M investing.

shifts.trend: Order book ~13,350 MWe Q2 FY25 → ~18,100 MWe Q3 FY25 (incl. +4,750 MWe new LOIs); Meta 1.2 GW deal Jan 2026 adds hyperscaler anchor
shifts.note: First hyperscaler prepayment for advanced fission is a category-defining demand signal

shifts.trend: NRC Phase I pre-application: no significant gaps; PDC topical report accepted Sep 2025; Aurora-INL DOE authorization granted; 3 of 11 DOE Reactor Pilot Program projects selected for Oklo
shifts.note: Regulatory de-risking is progressing faster than expected; NRC full application still pending

shifts.trend: FY26 investing activities guided $350-450M vs $606M investing in FY25 (weighted H1); Tennessee Advanced Fuel Center roadmap $1.68B investment with Kiewit
shifts.note: Capital requirements are escalating as the company moves from pre-application to construction phase

shifts.trend: Accumulated deficit $199.3M FY25; cash burn $139.3M opex FY25 vs $52.8M FY24 (+164%); equity financing ongoing (ATM at $88.29 avg, $300M raised Dec 2025)
shifts.note: Pre-revenue company with ~$430-550M total annual cash burn FY26 guided; runway depends on ongoing equity issuance

OKLO.verdict: OKLO is pre-revenue with a rapidly expanding order book. The Meta deal is transformative — first hyperscaler prepayment for advanced fission signals serious demand. Key risk is regulatory timeline (NRC full application not yet filed as of FY25 10-K). Thesis: advanced fission as a hyperscaler power solution is gaining traction; execution risk remains high.

ORCL.headline: OCI (cloud infrastructure) growth accelerated from +68% Q2 to +84% Q3; total RPO expanded from $523.3B to $552.6B; 9-month capex $39.2B (3x prior year); $42.7B senior notes + $5B convertible preferred issued.

shifts.trend: OCI +68% Q2 FY26 ($4.08B) → +84% Q3 FY26 ($4.89B); total revenue +14% → +22% as cloud mix expands
shifts.note: OCI is now the fastest-growing cloud infrastructure among major providers on a YoY basis

shifts.trend: RPO $523.3B Q2 FY26 → $552.6B Q3 FY26; both driven by significant multi-year cloud contract signings
shifts.note: RPO 4-5x current annual revenue; structural demand visibility unprecedented in ORCL history

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 capex $20.5B → 9M FY26 $39.2B (3x prior year pace); Q3 acceleration above H1 run-rate
shifts.note: Management committed to increasing capex 'next few fiscal years' — implies no near-term moderation

shifts.trend: $17.9B senior notes Sept 2025 + $24.8B Feb 2026 = $42.7B total notes issued; $5B mandatory convertible preferred also issued; stock repurchases minimal ($95M)
shifts.note: Leverage build is aggressive but backed by $552.6B RPO — debt-funded growth with concrete demand visibility

ORCL.verdict: OCI's +84% growth validates ORCL as the dark-horse hyperscaler with fastest cloud infrastructure growth. The $552B RPO represents committed revenue backlog that dwarfs current annual revenue — structural demand is secured. The leverage build ($42.7B notes) is the risk; as long as OCI growth sustains, the cash flow inflection story holds.

PLTR.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated from 53% FY25 (commercial) to 95% Q1 2026 (commercial); operating income $754M Q1 (+328% YoY); operating margin 46% Q1 vs ~31% FY25; US revenue expanding to 79% of total.

shifts.trend: FY25 total growth ~57% (gov +53%, commercial +60%) → Q1 2026 total +85% (gov +76%, commercial +95%)
shifts.note: Commercial surpassing government growth rate — signals PLTR is entering a broader enterprise adoption cycle

shifts.trend: Contribution margin 66% FY25 → 73% Q1 2026 (both gov and commercial); operating margin ~31% FY25 → 46% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Margin expansion at 85% growth rate is exceptional; SBC declining as % of revenue
US commercial emerging as the fastest-growing segment — AIP bootcamp strategy converting enterprise customers at scale

shifts.trend: No customer >10% of Q1 2026 or Q1 2025 revenue; US revenue 79% of total (vs 71% Q1 2025)
shifts.note: Diversifying end-customer base while deepening US government share

PLTR.verdict: Q1 2026 is a significant acceleration — 85% total growth with 46% operating margin at $1.63B revenue is well beyond consensus expectations. Commercial vs government dynamic shifting to commercial leadership carries higher long-run margins. Fundamental story is strengthening; the only constraint is valuation (elevated multiples on strong execution).

PWR.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +20.3% FY25 to +26.3% Q1 FY26; backlog reached $48.47B (+10.2% QoQ from $43.98B); RPO $26.24B (+10.4% QoQ); data center primary demand catalyst across both segments.

shifts.trend: FY25 +20.3% → Q1 FY26 +26.3%; Electric segment +$1.52B; operating income +42%
shifts.note: Both Electric and Underground segments citing data center as primary growth catalyst
Backlog growing faster than revenue — forward order acceleration implies further revenue re-acceleration

shifts.trend: Underground & Infrastructure margin 5.7% FY25 → Q1 FY26 operating margin improvement; Electric segment gross margin 14.1% Q1 vs 13.4% FY25
shifts.note: DSI (mechanical/plumbing/process infrastructure for data centers) and CEI acquisitions driving both Electric and Underground growth

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 13.4% → Q1 FY26 14.1% (+70bps); operating margin 3.8% → 4.3% (+50bps)
shifts.note: Modest but consistent margin improvement as scale and mix improve

PWR.verdict: PWR is the pure-play infrastructure builder for the AI power/data center buildout. Every dollar of hyperscaler capex flowing to US electrical grid goes through companies like PWR. $48.47B backlog growing +10% per quarter is outstanding visibility. Story intact — one of the most durable and broad-based beneficiaries in the power/grid layer.

SNOW.headline: Revenue growth stable/slight acceleration from +29% Q3 to +31% FY26; RPO expanded from $7.9B to $9.8B (+24%); customer count +21% and $1M+ customers +27%; NRR 125% (slight moderation from 126%).

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 product revenue $1.16B (+29%) → FY26 revenue $4.7B (+31%); $1M+ revenue customers 576 Q3 → 733 FY26 (+27%)
shifts.note: Slight acceleration from Q3 to FY26; large-customer cohort expanding faster than total revenue

shifts.trend: RPO $7.9B Q3 FY26 → $9.8B FY26 (+24%); 48% to recognize next 12 months (Q3) → 46% (FY26)
shifts.note: RPO growth outpacing revenue growth — pipeline building

shifts.trend: Customer count 10,996 Q3 → 13,328 FY26 (+21%); Forbes Global 2000 customers 790 = ~43% of revenue; NRR 125% vs 126%
shifts.note: Broader customer base with slightly moderating expansion rate; diversification improving

shifts.trend: Net loss maintained; SBC fell from ~41% to ~34% of revenue; operating cash flow $1.2B FY26
shifts.note: Path to profitability improving as revenue scales faster than SBC

SNOW.verdict: SNOW's stabilizing growth at 29-31% with RPO expanding and customer count surging represents solid execution on the AI/data infrastructure play. NRR at 125% is best-in-class but slight moderation (126% → 125%) is worth watching. Self-hosted public sector offering adds a new vector. Story intact as an AI/data infrastructure incumbent.

SNPS.headline: Revenue spiked from +15% FY25 to +66% Q1 FY26 from Ansys ($885.6M contribution); Design IP weak -6% in both periods; $3.5B term loan cleared Q1 FY26; Processor IP business sold to GlobalFoundries.

shifts.trend: FY25 +15% → Q1 FY26 +66%; Design Automation +96% Q1 FY26 (Ansys $885.6M of $953.5M incremental); organic growth still underlying
shifts.note: Ansys-driven step-function; underlying organic EDA growth intact but masked by Ansys scale

shifts.trend: Design IP -6% Q1 FY26 (also muted FY25 on BIS + foundry customer softness); Design Automation +96%; backlog $11.3B steady
shifts.note: IP weakness (BIS-driven, roadmap missteps, foundry softness) remains the primary drag; 2026 expected muted

shifts.trend: $13.5B total debt at FY25-end; $3.5B term loan paid off Q1 FY26; share repurchase suspended until debt paid down
shifts.note: Debt paydown accelerating; $10B Senior Notes remaining; buyback resumption is the next catalyst

shifts.trend: China -22% ex-Ansys FY25 on BIS export controls; continuing near-term headwind for Design IP in FY26
shifts.note: Unlike Cadence (BIS restrictions rescinded), Synopsys continues to cite China EDA headwinds in FY26 outlook

SNPS.verdict: Ansys integration is progressing. Design IP weakness is the near-term overhang. China headwind persists. Buyback resumption (after debt paydown) is the key catalyst. The EDA toll-booth compounder thesis holds; investment case requires patience on IP recovery and debt paydown over 12-24 months.

VRT.headline: Revenue growth stable at +27.7% FY25 → +30.1% Q1 FY26; backlog more than doubled from $7.2B to $15.0B in FY25 and Americas +53% Q1; gross margin expanded +400bps to 37.7%; CY26 capex guidance reaffirmed at $425-525M.

shifts.trend: FY25 +27.7% → Q1 FY26 +30.1%; Americas +41.9% FY25 → +53.1% Q1 FY26; Asia Pacific +14.9% and EMEA -20.3% Q1
shifts.note: Americas (62% of mix) driving the acceleration; EMEA declining but small relative to Americas

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $7.2B FY24 → $15.0B FY25 (doubled); Americas segment strong with major hyperscaler orders
shifts.note: Backlog doubling signals structural demand, not a spot order cycle

shifts.trend: Americas operating margin 26.8% FY25 → 27.0% Q1 FY26; consolidated GM 36.3% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 FY26 (+140bps QoQ)
shifts.note: Americas pricing power intact despite surging demand; mix and operating leverage driving the GM expansion

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → CY26 guided $425-525M (2x); Q1 FY26 capex $114M on pace for $456M annual
shifts.note: Capex doubling signals demand-confident investment in manufacturing capacity, not defensive spending

VRT.verdict: VRT's backlog doubling plus margin expansion is the ideal combination for a capital-intensive cooling infrastructure company. Americas at $1.81B Q1 (+53%) and 27% operating margin confirms pricing power amid surging demand. Capex doubling to $425-525M is demand-signaling investment. Story is the most intact and improving in the cooling layer.
